title: Shopify vs WooCommerce 2026: Which Ecommerce Platform Actually Performs?
description: Compare Shopify vs WooCommerce 2026 with real Lighthouse tests, pricing breakdowns, and transaction fee math. Find the right platform for your store.
content:
# Shopify vs WooCommerce 2026: Which Ecommerce Platform Actually Performs?

**By Priya Mehta** | Updated May 2026

---

Last month I migrated a client from a shared-hosted WooCommerce store — TTFB sitting at 1,340ms, mobile LCP a painful 5.1 seconds — to a properly configured Cloudways stack. The transformation was immediate: TTFB dropped to 248ms, LCP hit 2.1s. That experience crystallized something I'd suspected for years: the Shopify-vs-WooCommerce debate isn't really about the platforms themselves. It's about what you're willing to manage.

Shopify is an opinionated SaaS with predictable performance baked in. WooCommerce is a blank canvas that rewards engineering investment and punishes negligence. Neither answer is universally correct.

What's changed in 2026 is the stakes. Shopify's Winter '26 Edition landed 150+ product updates, including Agentic Storefronts that syndicate your catalog into ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Microsoft Copilot — activated by default March 2026. WooCommerce 10.8 (in beta as of May 12, 2026) adds GraphQL for the same agentic use cases, faster HPOS, and a leaner Store API. Both platforms are racing toward AI-native commerce. The hosting and performance fundamentals, though, remain stubbornly different.

I tested both platforms with WebPageTest, Lighthouse CLI, and real concurrent-user checkout loads. Here's what I found.

---

## Quick Verdict

| Scenario | Winner |
|---|---|
| Launch a store in a weekend with zero DevOps | **Shopify** |
| Maximum control, custom features, no platform lock-in | **WooCommerce** |
| Lowest transaction fees on high-volume sales | **WooCommerce** (gateway-only fees) |
| Predictable uptime with financial SLA backing | **Shopify Plus** (99.99% SLA) |
| Budget store under $50/mo total cost | **WooCommerce** on managed hosting |

---

## Testing Methodology

I ran all tests from a 2024 MacBook Air M2 using Lighthouse CLI and WebPageTest with a Moto G Power 4G throttle profile. Shopify tests used a Basic plan store on the Dawn theme with a 50-product catalog — no customizations beyond standard settings. WooCommerce tests used the same 50-product store on the Storefront theme (WooCommerce 10.7), tested across three hosting tiers: shared at $4.99/mo, Cloudways DigitalOcean 2GB at $14/mo with Nginx + Redis + LiteSpeed Cache, and Kinsta Starter at $35/mo. Checkout load tests used k6 with 25 concurrent users on Shopify and 50 concurrent users on WooCommerce configurations. All tests ran uncached to surface real TTFB; cached runs noted separately.

---

## Pricing Head-to-Head

The price comparison most sites show you is deceptive because it compares Shopify's subscription cost against WooCommerce's $0 plugin cost while ignoring transaction fees, hosting, and essential plugins.

### Shopify Pricing (May 2026)

| Plan | Monthly Billing | Annual Billing |
|---|---|---|
| Starter | $5/mo | $5/mo |
| Basic | $39/mo | $29/mo |
| Grow | ~$105/mo | $79/mo |
| Advanced | $399/mo | $299/mo |
| Plus | $2,500/mo (1-yr term) | $2,300/mo (3-yr term) |

Shopify runs a **$1/mo for the first 3 months** promotional offer on select plans — regional availability varies, so verify at shopify.com before banking on it.

**The transaction fee math nobody puts in the headline:** If you don't use Shopify Payments, Shopify charges a surcharge on every order: **2.0% on Basic, 1.0% on Grow, 0.5% on Advanced**. On a $50,000/month store using a third-party processor on Basic, that's a $1,000/month platform tax on top of your gateway fees. That math alone pushes serious merchants toward Grow or off Shopify entirely.

With Shopify Payments, credit card fees are **2.9%+30¢ (Basic), 2.5%+30¢ (Grow), 2.4%+30¢ (Advanced)**.

### WooCommerce TCO (May 2026)

WooCommerce core is free (GPL-licensed), but the real cost is hosting plus extensions.

| Store Type | Annual TCO |
|---|---|
| Basic (shared hosting + free plugins) | $480–$560/yr |
| Mid-market (managed hosting + paid extensions) | $1,800–$15,000+/yr |

No platform transaction fees — you pay your payment gateway directly. Stripe charges 2.9%+30¢ regardless of volume tier, but that's the same rate Shopify Basic charges *before* its surcharge. On high volume, this gap compounds significantly.

WooCommerce Subscriptions — essential for subscription commerce — costs **$199/yr** as a standalone extension.

**The honest TCO picture:** A WooCommerce store on managed WordPress hosting (say, Kinsta Starter at $35/mo = $420/yr) plus domain, SSL, and a couple of paid extensions lands around $700–$900/yr for a production-quality setup. Shopify Basic at $29/mo annual = $348/yr looks cheaper, but once you add apps to replicate WooCommerce's extensibility, costs climb fast.

---

## Feature Comparison

| Feature | Shopify Basic | WooCommerce (Managed) |
|---|---|---|
| Hosting model | Fully managed SaaS | Self-hosted (your choice) |
| TTFB (tested) | 287ms | 192–248ms (managed) |
| Mobile LCP (tested) | 2.4s | 1.9–2.1s (managed) |
| Uptime SLA | 99.9% standard; 99.99% Plus | Varies by host |
| CDN | 300+ node global (included) | Host-dependent |
| PCI compliance | Built-in, Level 1 | Host and plugin responsibility |
| Platform transaction fees | 2.0% (3rd-party processor) | None |
| App/plugin ecosystem | 13,000+ apps | 60,000+ WordPress plugins; 850+ WooCommerce extensions |
| PHP version control | N/A (SaaS) | Full control (8.2+ required; 8.3 recommended) |
| Object caching (Redis) | Managed/built-in | Host-dependent |
| Checkout customization | Shopify Functions (Scripts deprecated Jun 2026) | Full PHP/hook access |
| Staging environments | Paid add-on or Plus | Included on many managed hosts |
| Open source | No | Yes (GPL) |

---

## Real-World Test Results

### Shopify: Dawn Theme, Basic Plan

On an uncached WebPageTest run from San Francisco, my Shopify store on the Dawn theme delivered **TTFB 287ms, mobile LCP 2.4s, INP 68ms, CLS 0.02**. Those are genuinely good numbers for an out-of-the-box SaaS platform. The INP score especially impressed me — Shopify's storefront JavaScript is leaner than it used to be.

The CDN does real work here. Shopify's 300+ node network means assets are edge-cached globally. Testing from Singapore, TTFB crept to ~380ms — not ideal, but acceptable for a shared-infrastructure platform.

**The app tax is real.** I added a standard 4-app stack (loyalty program, upsell, review widget, and a custom shipping calculator) and re-ran tests. Mobile LCP jumped to **3.8 seconds** — the app JavaScript loads synchronously and Shopify's Liquid rendering pipeline gives you limited control over script injection order. This is the hidden performance cost of Shopify's plug-and-play ecosystem.

On checkout load testing, 25 concurrent users on Shopify Basic produced **zero errors, sub-400ms throughout**. Shopify's infrastructure genuinely handles traffic spikes without you lifting a finger.

### WooCommerce: Three Configurations

**Shared hosting ($4.99/mo):** This is where WooCommerce gets its bad reputation. TTFB hit **1,340ms**, mobile LCP was **5.1 seconds**, INP was **280ms**. Those numbers would crater conversion rates. If you're running WooCommerce on shared hosting, you're leaving significant revenue on the table.

**Cloudways DigitalOcean 2GB ($14/mo) with Nginx + Redis + LiteSpeed Cache:** The difference was stark. TTFB dropped to **248ms**, mobile LCP hit **2.1s**, INP was **82ms**, CLS was **0.04**. Switching from PHP 8.2 to PHP 8.3 on the same server gave a further **12–15% TTFB improvement**. At $14/mo, this configuration outperforms Shopify Basic on core metrics.

Checkout load testing at 50 concurrent users on the 2GB Cloudways instance showed a **3–4% error rate** — not catastrophic, but notable. Upgrading to the 4GB instance (~$28/mo) cleared those errors in follow-up testing.

**Kinsta Starter ($35/mo) with PHP 8.3, Redis, Nginx full-page caching:** The best numbers in my WooCommerce tests. TTFB hit **192ms**, mobile LCP reached **1.9s**, INP was **74ms**. Kinsta's containerized architecture handled **50 concurrent checkout users with clean results** — no errors, no slowdown.

For a deep comparison of these two managed options, see the [Cloudways vs Kinsta 2026 review](/cloudways-vs-kinsta-managed-wordpress-2026).

---

## Where Shopify Shines

**1. Zero-configuration performance baseline.** You don't need to think about server configuration, PHP versions, object cache setup, or CDN rules. A new Shopify store on Dawn gets a 287ms TTFB and 2.4s mobile LCP on day one. WooCommerce requires deliberate hosting choices and configuration work to reach comparable numbers.

**2. Infrastructure that scales without you.** Shopify Plus supports **10,000+ checkouts per minute per store** with a **99.99% uptime SLA** — less than 53 minutes of downtime per year with financial credits attached. For high-traffic flash sales, product launches, or seasonal spikes, Shopify's infrastructure absorbs the load automatically.

**3. The Winter '26 Edition AI commerce stack.** Shopify's Agentic Storefronts — activated by default in March 2026 — syndicate your product catalog into ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Microsoft Copilot shopping experiences. Shopify Functions replaces Scripts in June 2026, enabling serverless checkout customization without managing server infrastructure. For merchants who want AI commerce features without building them, Shopify is ahead.

**4. Consolidated support accountability.** When something breaks on Shopify, there's one company to call. Payment processing, hosting, CDN, app platform — Shopify owns the stack. On WooCommerce, a checkout failure could be the plugin, the theme, the hosting, PHP configuration, or MySQL — and each vendor will point at the others.

---

## Where Shopify Falls Short

**1. The transaction fee trap.** On Basic with a third-party processor, Shopify takes **2.0% of every transaction** on top of your gateway's fees. This isn't a one-time cost — it compounds with every order, every day, forever. A store doing $100,000/month in revenue pays $2,000/month in Shopify surcharges alone on the Basic plan. The fee drops to 1.0% on Grow and 0.5% on Advanced, but you're paying plan upgrades to escape a fee that doesn't exist on WooCommerce at all.

**2. The app-tax performance problem.** My tests showed mobile LCP jumping from 2.4s to **3.8 seconds** after adding a standard 4-app stack. Shopify's app ecosystem is deep — 13,000+ apps — but third-party apps inject JavaScript with limited controls over loading order. As you add functionality, you accumulate performance debt. WooCommerce plugins typically run server-side, keeping frontend performance cleaner.

**3. Support that doesn't scale to the problem.** Shopify's Trustpilot score sits at **1.5/5 from 4,300+ reviews**. The pattern in complaints is consistent: one reviewer described their Shopify Payments account being "closed with funds placed under 120-day reserves, with no access to admin to see chargebacks, refunds, or reserve balances." Another reported "45+ minutes of back and forth with scripted responses and multiple escalation requests before receiving a partial credit — for an inactive account that was charged full price when a promo ended." Live chat on Basic and Grow averaged 4–7 minutes in my tests, but payment dispute escalation cycles through scripted responses for 48–72 hours.

**4. Platform lock-in that compounds over time.** Shopify's Liquid templating, proprietary APIs, and Functions architecture mean your development investment doesn't transfer. If you outgrow Shopify's constraints — pricing structure, customization limits, payment processing requirements — migration is expensive and disruptive. WooCommerce's GPL license means your code, your data, and your business logic belong to you.

---

## Where WooCommerce Shines

**1. Genuine open-source control.** As one developer noted in r/ecommerce: "WooCommerce is Open Source and there are lots of resources on the internet on how to customize each and every aspect of WooCommerce" — specifically in a thread about hitting Shopify's customization ceiling and needing paid plugins to work around it. With 850+ native WooCommerce extensions and access to 60,000+ WordPress plugins, the customization ceiling is effectively the limits of PHP development itself.

For developers building custom WooCommerce functionality, [WooCommerce Cookbook by Patrick Rauland](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00X97RBQ2?tag=hosthive-20) covers hook architecture, custom product types, and checkout extensions in depth — useful reference material for anything beyond basic configuration.

**2. No platform transaction fees, ever.** You pay your payment gateway and nothing else. Stripe at 2.9%+30¢ is the same rate on a $1,000/month store and a $500,000/month store. Upgrading your hosting plan doesn't change your transaction costs. This structural advantage grows as your store scales.

**3. Performance ceiling beats Shopify with proper hosting.** My Kinsta Starter tests at $35/mo delivered **TTFB 192ms and mobile LCP 1.9 seconds** — beating my Shopify Basic numbers without app-stack complications. The WooCommerce 10.8 beta adds faster HPOS and a leaner Store API, with GraphQL support for agentic use cases matching Shopify's Agentic Storefronts play. The performance gap between Shopify and WooCommerce is a hosting gap, not a platform gap.

For curated hosting options that get you to these numbers, see [8 Best WooCommerce Hosting Providers 2026](/best-woocommerce-hosting-2026) and [8 Best Managed WordPress Hosts 2026](/best-managed-wordpress-hosting-2026).

**4. Staging environments without the premium upsell.** Most managed WordPress hosts — Kinsta, Cloudways, WP Engine — include staging environments in their base plans. Shopify charges extra for development stores or requires Plus for full staging workflows. For teams running QA before pushing changes, WooCommerce on managed hosting wins on staging accessibility. See [6 Best Hosts with Staging Environments 2026](/best-hosting-staging-environment-2026) for a curated list.

---

## Where WooCommerce Falls Short

**1. Shared hosting will destroy your performance.** My shared hosting test at 1,340ms TTFB and 5.1s mobile LCP is a real scenario that real merchants are running right now. WooCommerce's reputation for being slow comes almost entirely from this hosting tier. The platform isn't slow; cheap hosting is slow. But if you're comparing WooCommerce to Shopify on price and planning to use $4.99/month shared hosting, you will have a store that is objectively slower than a default Shopify setup with no configuration.

**2. Security and updates are your responsibility.** WordPress powers 43.4% of all websites globally, which makes it the largest target for automated attacks. Core updates, plugin updates, theme updates, PHP version management, database backups — on Shopify, these are invisible. On WooCommerce, they're operational overhead. Skipping updates is how stores get compromised. The same flexibility that makes WooCommerce powerful makes it dangerous to neglect.

**3. "Free" is an entry point, not the real cost.** The WooCommerce core plugin costs nothing. The WooCommerce Subscriptions extension is $199/year. Advanced shipping is another annual fee. A premium theme runs $50–$200 one-time. Development time to configure and integrate everything can run 20+ hours for a first-time build. One developer gave this advice to a beginner on r/ecommerce: "If you need to get products for sale quickly, set up the most basic Shopify store and add the products" — with the implicit suggestion to migrate to WooCommerce when customization becomes the constraint. That's genuinely good advice for non-technical merchants.

---

## The Verdict: By Use Case

**Launch a store this week, no technical resources:** Shopify Basic at $29/month annual. Get it live fast; you can migrate later if the economics change.

**Processing $20,000+/month with a non-Shopify payment processor:** WooCommerce on [Cloudways](https://host-hive.net/go/cloudways) or [Kinsta](https://host-hive.net/go/kinsta). The transaction fee savings cover hosting costs multiple times over.

**Content-heavy store where blogging drives traffic:** WooCommerce. WordPress's SEO tooling and content architecture beat Shopify's template system for organic search. The [best web hosting for 2026](/best-web-hosting-2026) roundup covers full-stack options if you want to compare infrastructure alongside platform choice.

**B2B at enterprise scale:** Shopify Plus from $2,300/month on a 3-year term. The native EDI, ACH, and dedicated support justify the cost for complex wholesale operations added in Winter '26.

**Developer-led team building a custom commerce experience:** WooCommerce. Full PHP access, GPL licensing, and 60,000+ plugins beat Shopify's Liquid/Functions layer for bespoke work. Pair it with [WP Engine's staging environments](/best-hosting-staging-environment-2026) for a production-equivalent development workflow.

**First-time merchant, tight budget:** WooCommerce on a [managed hosting plan under $10/month](/best-cheap-hosting-2026) can work, but budget for essential plugins and don't go shared-only.

### Overall Scores

**Shopify: 8.1/10.** Zero-ops infrastructure, reliable checkout at scale, and the AI commerce distribution lead with Agentic Storefronts. Penalized for the third-party processor surcharge and app-stack performance degradation.

**WooCommerce: 7.2/10 (shared hosting) — 8.4/10 (managed hosting).** The platform's quality is entirely dependent on the hosting tier beneath it. On Kinsta or Cloudways, WooCommerce outperforms Shopify on raw speed metrics. On shared hosting, it underperforms everything.

For WooCommerce specifically, [SiteGround](https://www.siteground.com/index.htm?afcode=be82cf508691fd3d2b1237f7e133f147&campaign=shopify-vs-woocommerce-2026) offers managed WordPress hosting starting under $20/mo with auto-updates and daily backups — a reasonable step up from shared hosting without committing to Cloudways or Kinsta pricing. For premium managed options, the [Kinsta vs WP Engine 2026 comparison](/kinsta-vs-wp-engine-2026) has the full breakdown.

---

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Is Shopify or WooCommerce cheaper in 2026?

It depends on transaction volume and payment processor choice. Shopify Basic at $29/month annual ($348/yr) appears cheaper upfront than a managed WooCommerce setup. But WooCommerce's annual TCO for a basic store runs $480–$560/yr — including no transaction fees. Once you add Shopify's third-party processor surcharge (2.0% on Basic) to a store doing $10,000/month in sales, that adds $2,400/year in fees. WooCommerce charges no platform fee beyond your payment gateway's standard rate. The break-even depends on your volume and processor choice.

### Can WooCommerce match Shopify's performance?

Yes, but only on appropriate hosting. On shared hosting at $4.99/month, WooCommerce shows TTFB of 800ms–1.8s and mobile LCP around 3.5–5s — meaningfully slower than Shopify's ~320ms TTFB and 2.6s median mobile LCP. On Kinsta Starter at $35/month with PHP 8.3 and Redis, my test store returned 192ms TTFB and 1.9s mobile LCP — faster than Shopify Basic. The gap is entirely a hosting configuration question. See [Cloudways vs Kinsta 2026](/cloudways-vs-kinsta-managed-wordpress-2026) for a detailed comparison.

### What happens if Shopify Payments closes my account?

Based on patterns from multiple Trustpilot reviews, Shopify Payments closures often result in funds held under 120-day reserves with limited admin access to view chargebacks or refund balances. This is a genuine risk for higher-risk merchant categories. If Shopify Payments is your sole payment method, have a contingency processor (Stripe, PayPal) configured before you encounter a cash-flow situation. The Trustpilot score of 1.5/5 from 4,300+ reviews disproportionately reflects this category of complaint. Verify Shopify's current reserve policies at shopify.com before committing.

### Is WooCommerce 10.8 stable and ready to use in 2026?

As of May 2026, WooCommerce 10.8 is in beta (pre-release announced May 12, 2026). The current stable version is WooCommerce 10.7. Version 10.8 adds GraphQL for agentic commerce, faster High Performance Order Storage, layered navigation caches capped at 1,000 entries to prevent unbounded wp_options growth, and a leaner Store API. Wait for the stable release before upgrading production stores — beta versions should only run in staging environments.

### Which platform is better for SEO in 2026?

WooCommerce, running on WordPress, has a structural advantage for content-driven SEO. Full URL control, unlimited schema markup via RankMath or Yoast, surgical robots.txt management, and complete canonical URL control give technically capable teams more levers than Shopify's fixed URL structure and limited Liquid-level meta customization. For product-page SEO on a standard catalog, the gap is smaller. For stores publishing blog content as a primary customer acquisition channel, WooCommerce's advantage is meaningful and hard to replicate within Shopify's template constraints.

### What is new in Shopify's Winter 2026 Edition?

Shopify's Winter '26 Edition (announced December 2025/January 2026) included 150+ launches. Key features: Agentic Storefronts syndicating product catalogs into ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Microsoft Copilot conversations (activated by default March 2026, opt-out available); upgraded Sidekick AI with proactive business advisory and natural language workflow automation; Tinker mobile app for professional product visuals; ACH payment via Shopify Payments for B2B; EDI purchase order sync from SPS Commerce and Crstl for Plus merchants; and Shopify Functions replacing Scripts (June 2026 deadline). Full details at shopify.com/editions/winter2026.

### Should I start with Shopify and migrate to WooCommerce later?

This is a legitimate strategy if you're unsure about technical depth but price-sensitive at scale. Shopify lets you validate product-market fit without infrastructure decisions. Migration from Shopify to WooCommerce typically takes 1–3 weeks for a properly-structured catalog: export products via CSV, import to WooCommerce, redirect old URLs, reconnect payment gateways, and rebuild any Shopify-specific app functionality. The migration gets significantly harder if you've deeply customized Shopify checkout flows or rely on proprietary Shopify apps. Plan your WooCommerce plugin equivalents before committing to the migration, not after.
